content:
# Acts Chapter 14 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 242-243 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_014_page_001.jpg, ACT_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass in Iconium that they entered together into the synagogue of the Jews and spoke in such a way that a great multitude of both Jews and Greeks believed.

**2** But the Jews who were disobedient stirred up and poisoned the minds of the Gentiles against the brothers.

**3** Therefore they stayed a considerable time, speaking boldly in the Lord, who bore witness to the word of his grace, granting signs and wonders to be done through their hands.

**4** But the multitude of the city was divided, and some were with the Jews, and some with the apostles.

**5** And when there was an assault by both the Gentiles and the Jews, together with their rulers, to mistreat and to stone them,

**6** having become aware of it, they fled to the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra and Derbe, and the surrounding region,

**7** and there they were proclaiming the gospel.

**8** And a certain man in Lystra, powerless in his feet, was sitting, lame from his mother's womb, who had never walked.

**9** This man was hearing Paul speaking, who, having looked intently at him and having seen that he had faith to be saved,

**10** said with a loud voice, "Stand upright on your feet!" And he leaped up and began to walk.

**11** And when the crowds saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voices in the Lycaonian language, saying, "The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!"

**12** And they called Barnabas Zeus, and Paul Hermes, since he was the chief speaker.

**13** And the priest of Zeus, whose temple was before the city, having brought bulls and garlands to the gates, wished to offer sacrifice together with the crowds.

**14** But when the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard of it, having torn their garments, they rushed out into the crowd, crying out

**15** and saying, "Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men of like nature with you, proclaiming the gospel to you, that you should turn from these vain things to the living God, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and all things that are in them,

**16** who in the generations gone by permitted all the nations to go their own ways;

**17** and yet he did not leave himself without witness, doing good and giving you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling your hearts with food and gladness."

**18** And saying these things, they scarcely restrained the crowds from offering sacrifice to them.

**19** But Jews came from Antioch and Iconium, and having persuaded the crowds and having stoned Paul, they dragged him outside the city, supposing him to be dead.

**20** But when the disciples surrounded him, he rose up and entered into the city. And on the next day he went out with Barnabas to Derbe.

**21** And having proclaimed the gospel to that city and having made many disciples, they returned to Lystra and to Iconium and to Antioch,

**22** strengthening the souls of the disciples, exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that through many tribulations we must enter into the kingdom of God.

**23** And having appointed for them elders in every church, having prayed with fasting, they committed them to the Lord in whom they had believed.

**24** And having passed through Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia.

**25** And having spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia,

**26** and from there they sailed to Antioch, from where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work which they had fulfilled.

**27** And having arrived and having gathered the church together, they reported all the things that God had done with them, and that he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles.

**28** And they stayed no little time with the disciples.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 14 continues the first missionary journey of Paul and Barnabas, begun in chapter 13 with the commissioning at Antioch and the ministry in Cyprus and Pisidian Antioch. The chapter traces a dramatic arc: initial success in Iconium (vv. 1-7), the healing miracle and its pagan misinterpretation at Lystra (vv. 8-18), the stoning of Paul (v. 19), and the return journey through the newly established churches (vv. 20-28). The chapter is structured around a sharp theological irony: the apostles who refuse divine honors in Lystra (vv. 14-15) are then stoned as criminals by the same populace (v. 19), recapitulating the pattern of Jesus' own reception -- exaltation followed by violent rejection. The return journey (vv. 21-28) shifts from missionary proclamation to ecclesiological consolidation: appointing elders, strengthening disciples, and reporting to the sending church at Antioch. This forms a narrative inclusio with Acts 13:1-3, completing the first missionary circuit.

**Manuscript condition:** Acts 14 begins in the third column of page 242 (visible at bottom of page 001 image) and continues through page 243 (page 002 image) into the first columns. The text is well-preserved throughout. Standard nomina sacra are used: ΘΥ/ΘΣ (God), ΚΥ/ΚΣ (Lord). The critical variant in verse 9 (ΣΩΘΗΝΑΙ vs. ΙΑΘΗΝΑΙ) is clearly legible in Sinaiticus as ΣΩΘΗΝΑΙ. The transition from Acts 14 to Acts 15 is visible in the fourth column of page 243.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show substantial agreement on the overall content and narrative flow. The primary points of divergence concern: (1) the critical variant in v. 9 (sothenai "to be saved" vs. iathenai "to be healed"), (2) the rendering of apeithesantes in v. 2 (disobedient vs. unbelieving), (3) the Zeus/Hermes identification and its phrasing in v. 12, (4) cheirotonēsantes in v. 23 (the mode of appointing elders), (5) various lexical choices in the Lystra speech (vv. 15-17), and (6) the rendering of key participial chains throughout.

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as the formal equivalence approach best serves the narrative's blend of historical detail and theological significance. EMET's literal renderings are consulted for key morphological decisions (particularly the participial chains in vv. 19-27 and the textual variant in v. 9). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings inform the consensus where clarity and vividness serve the text without sacrificing precision (particularly in the Lystra narrative, vv. 8-18).

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 1 -- "according to the same [pattern]" vs. "together" vs. "the same thing happened":** EMET renders kata to auto as "according-to the same [pattern]"; LOGOS renders "together" (taking kata to auto as an adverbial phrase); PNEUMA renders freely as "the same thing happened." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "together" renders the phrase as an adverb modifying "entered," which is the more natural Greek reading -- they entered the synagogue together, as a pair. EMET's "according to the same pattern" is a defensible alternative reading, and PNEUMA's paraphrase captures the implied meaning (the same pattern as in Pisidian Antioch) but adds what is not stated explicitly.

2. **Verse 2 -- "disobedient" (apeithesantes):** All three translators agree on the core meaning. EMET renders "disobedient"; LOGOS renders "who were disobedient"; PNEUMA renders "who refused to believe." The Sinaiticus reads ΑΠΕΙΘΗΣΑΝΤΕΣ, an aorist participle from apeitheō, which means both "to disobey" and "to disbelieve" (the two concepts are inseparable in biblical Greek -- disobedience is the expression of disbelief). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "the Jews who were disobedient" preserves the aorist sense (a decisive act) while maintaining the primary meaning of the verb. PNEUMA's "refused to believe" captures the belief dimension but loses the obedience dimension.

3. **Verse 3 -- "upon the Lord" vs. "in the Lord":** EMET renders epi to kyrio as "upon the Lord"; LOGOS renders "in the Lord"; PNEUMA renders "for the Lord." The preposition epi with the dative can mean "on the basis of," "in reliance upon," or "in connection with." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "in the Lord" captures the sense that their bold speech was grounded in the Lord's authority and presence, without the spatial awkwardness of "upon."

4. **Verse 5 -- "impulse" vs. "assault" vs. "plot":** EMET renders horme as "impulse"; LOGOS renders "assault"; PNEUMA renders "plot." The Greek horme means a rush, onset, or violent impulse -- it is more spontaneous than a "plot" (which implies premeditation) and more threatening than a mere "impulse" (which sounds psychological). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "assault" conveys the hostile, physical nature of the threat while allowing for the spontaneous element.

5. **Verse 6 -- "fled-down" vs. "fled" vs. "escaped":** EMET renders katephygon as "fled-down" (preserving the kata- prefix indicating geographical descent); LOGOS renders "fled"; PNEUMA renders "escaped." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "fled" is sufficient, as the geographical note is conveyed by the destination ("cities of Lycaonia"). EMET's compound rendering is informative but over-specifies in English.

6. **Verse 8 -- Description of the lame man:** All three translators convey the essential details: powerlessness in feet, lifelong condition, never walked. PNEUMA adds "never walked a step in his life" for dramatic emphasis. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "powerless in his feet, was sitting, lame from his mother's womb, who had never walked" -- this preserves Luke's careful stacking of descriptors that establish the magnitude of the coming miracle.

7. **Verse 9 -- sothenai ("to be saved") vs. iathenai ("to be healed") -- MAJOR VARIANT:** See the Disagreement Log for full discussion. EMET and LOGOS read "to be saved" (sothenai), following the Sinaiticus reading ΣΩΘΗΝΑΙ. PNEUMA reads "to be healed" (iathenai), following later manuscripts. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS and the Sinaiticus: "to be saved." This is a theologically significant variant -- the Sinaiticus reading expands the man's faith beyond physical healing to encompass salvation broadly, which is the richer Lukan meaning.

8. **Verse 10 -- "was walking" vs. "walked" vs. "began to walk":** EMET renders the imperfect periepatei as "was walking" (ongoing action); LOGOS renders "walked" (simple past); PNEUMA renders "began to walk" (ingressive). SOPHIA combines: "began to walk" -- the imperfect verb here has an ingressive force (the beginning of a new action), and PNEUMA's rendering captures this nuance well. The man who had never walked began walking -- the ingressive sense heightens the miracle.

9. **Verse 11 -- "in-Lycaonian" vs. "in the Lycaonian language":** EMET preserves the adverb Lykaonisti as "in-Lycaonian"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "in the Lycaonian language." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "in the Lycaonian language" is more natural in English and preserves the narrative significance -- the crowd spoke in their local dialect, which is why Paul and Barnabas did not immediately grasp the divine identification.

10. **Verse 12 -- "the [one] leading the word" vs. "the chief speaker" vs. "the one who did the talking":** EMET renders ho hēgoumenos tou logou as "the [one] leading the word"; LOGOS renders "the chief speaker"; PNEUMA renders "the one who did the talking." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "the chief speaker" -- this is a compact, dignified rendering. EMET's literal translation, while accurate, is awkward in English. PNEUMA's "did the talking" is too colloquial for the Lukan register.

11. **Verse 13 -- "bulls" vs. "oxen" vs. "bulls":** EMET and PNEUMA render taurous as "bulls"; LOGOS renders "oxen." The Greek tauros means specifically "bull" (a male bovine), not the generic "oxen." SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "bulls" is the precise rendering. However, SOPHIA also incorporates EMET's "together with the crowds" (syn tois ochlois), which LOGOS renders "with the crowds" and PNEUMA paraphrases as "he and the crowds." The phrase modifies the priest's intention -- he wished to offer sacrifice together with the assembled crowds.

12. **Verse 15 -- "Men" vs. "Friends":** EMET and LOGOS render andres as "Men"; PNEUMA renders "Friends." The Greek andres is the standard address in Acts (cf. 2:14, 3:12, 13:16) and means "men" specifically. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1): "Men" preserves the Greek term. PNEUMA's "Friends" is an interpretive substitution that softens the urgency of the address.

13. **Verse 15 -- "of-like-nature" vs. "of like nature" vs. "just like you":** EMET renders homoiopatheis as "of-like-nature"; LOGOS renders "of like nature"; PNEUMA renders "only human beings, just like you." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "of like nature" -- the Greek homoiopatheis means "subject to the same experiences/passions," a philosophical term used to emphasize shared humanity. PNEUMA's doubling ("only human beings, just like you") captures the sentiment but departs from the single-word concept.

14. **Verse 17 -- "without-witness" vs. "without witness" vs. "without evidence of who he is":** EMET and LOGOS render amartyron as "without witness"; PNEUMA expands to "without evidence of who he is." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "without witness" preserves the compact Greek negative adjective. PNEUMA's expansion, while illuminating, adds interpretive material not in the text.

15. **Verse 19 -- "supposing him to have died" vs. "supposing him to be dead" vs. "leaving him for dead":** EMET renders nomisantes auton tethnēkenai as "supposing him to have died" (preserving the perfect infinitive); LOGOS renders "supposing him to be dead"; PNEUMA renders "leaving him for dead." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "supposing him to be dead" is natural English that preserves the key detail -- they thought he was dead but were not certain. PNEUMA's "leaving him for dead" is more idiomatic but implies intentional abandonment rather than mistaken assumption.

16. **Verse 22 -- "remain" vs. "continue" vs. "hold firm":** EMET renders emmenein as "remain"; LOGOS renders "continue"; PNEUMA renders "hold firm." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "continue" -- the verb emmenō means to remain in, abide in, or continue in. "Continue" best captures the ongoing, persistent sense without the spatial connotation of "remain" or the combative tone of "hold firm."

17. **Verse 23 -- cheirotonēsantes ("having appointed"):** All three translators agree on "appointed." See the Disagreement Log for discussion of the term's specific meaning and ecclesiological significance.

18. **Verse 26 -- "delivered-over" vs. "commended" vs. "entrusted":** EMET renders paradedomenoi as "delivered-over"; LOGOS renders "commended"; PNEUMA renders "entrusted." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "commended" -- the verb paradidōmi has a wide semantic range (hand over, deliver, entrust, commend). In this context, the church had commended Paul and Barnabas to God's grace for their missionary work. "Commended" captures the formal, liturgical character of the sending (cf. 13:3). EMET's "delivered-over" preserves the root sense but sounds punitive in English. PNEUMA's "entrusted" is acceptable but shifts the agency -- they were commended (by the church) to God's grace, not entrusted (by God) to something.

19. **Verse 27 -- "as-many-things-as" vs. "all the things that" vs. "everything":** EMET renders hosa as "as-many-things-as"; LOGOS renders "all the things that"; PNEUMA renders "everything." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "all the things that" -- this preserves the correlative sense of hosa without EMET's hyphenated awkwardness or PNEUMA's brevity.

20. **Verse 28 -- "not a little" vs. "no little" vs. "a considerable":** EMET renders ouk oligon as "not a little"; LOGOS renders "no little"; PNEUMA renders "a considerable." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "no little time" -- this preserves the Lukan litotes (understatement by negation), which is a characteristic literary feature. PNEUMA's "a considerable time" resolves the litotes into a positive statement, losing the stylistic marker.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "according to the same" vs. "together" vs. "the same thing happened" | "according-to the same [pattern]" | "together" | "the same thing happened" | "together" -- follows LOGOS; kata to auto as adverb |
| 1 | "it happened" vs. "it came to pass" vs. omitted | "it happened" | "it came to pass" | omitted | "it came to pass" -- follows LOGOS; preserves egeneto |
| 2 | "embittered the souls" vs. "poisoned the minds" vs. "poisoned their minds" | "embittered the souls" | "poisoned the minds" | "poisoned their minds" | "poisoned the minds" -- follows LOGOS; ekakōsan = made evil/hostile; "poisoned the minds" captures the malice; EMET's "embittered the souls" uses psychas literally but "minds" conveys the target of the propaganda |
| 3 | "speaking-boldly upon the Lord" vs. "speaking boldly in the Lord" vs. "speaking boldly for the Lord" | "upon the Lord" (epi) | "in the Lord" | "for the Lord" | "in the Lord" -- follows LOGOS; epi + dative = on the basis of / in; see Council Notes |
| 3 | "the [one] bearing-witness" construction | Participial with article | "who bore witness" | "who confirmed the message" | "who bore witness" -- follows LOGOS; martyrounti = bearing witness; PNEUMA's "confirmed" interprets rather than translates |
| 5 | "impulse" vs. "assault" vs. "plot" | "impulse" (horme) | "assault" | "plot" | "assault" -- follows LOGOS; horme = rush/onset; see Council Notes |
| 6 | "fled-down" vs. "fled" vs. "escaped" | "fled-down" (katephygon) | "fled" | "escaped" | "fled" -- follows LOGOS; kata- prefix is geographical, not needed in English |
| 7 | "evangelizing" vs. "proclaiming the gospel" vs. "spread the good news" | "evangelizing" | "proclaiming the gospel" | "spread the good news" | "proclaiming the gospel" -- follows LOGOS; euangelizomenoi rendered as verbal phrase |
| 8 | "powerless [in his] feet, was sitting" -- construction | Literal participial | Smooth formal | Expanded dramatic | Follows LOGOS structure; see Council Notes |
| **9** | **sothenai ("saved") vs. iathenai ("healed") -- MAJOR VARIANT** | "to be saved" (sothenai) | "to be saved" (sothenai) | "to be healed" (iathenai) | **"to be saved"** -- 2-to-1; Sinaiticus reads ΣΩΘΗΝΑΙ; see detailed discussion below |
| 9 | "has faith" (present) vs. "had faith" (past) | "has faith" (echei, present) | "had faith" (past rendering) | "had faith" (past rendering) | "had faith" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); English past tense in narrative sequence; the Greek present is a vivid/historical present |
| 10 | "was walking" vs. "walked" vs. "began to walk" | "was walking" (imperfect) | "walked" | "began to walk" | "began to walk" -- follows PNEUMA; ingressive imperfect; see Council Notes |
| 11 | "in-Lycaonian" vs. "in the Lycaonian language" | "in-Lycaonian" | "in the Lycaonian language" | "in the Lycaonian language" | "in the Lycaonian language" -- 2-to-1; natural English |
| 11 | "having been made-like men" vs. "in the likeness of men" vs. "in human form" | "having been made-like men" | "in the likeness of men" | "in human form" | "in the likeness of men" -- follows LOGOS; homoiōthentes anthrōpois; PNEUMA's "human form" loses the participle's theological echo |
| 12 | "the [one] leading the word" vs. "the chief speaker" vs. "the one who did the talking" | "the [one] leading the word" | "the chief speaker" | "the one who did the talking" | "the chief speaker" -- follows LOGOS; see Council Notes |
| 13 | "bulls" vs. "oxen" | "bulls" (taurous) | "oxen" | "bulls" | "bulls" -- 2-to-1; tauros = bull specifically |
| 13 | "the [one] being before the city" vs. "whose temple was before the city" vs. "whose temple stood just outside the city" | "the [one] being before the city" | "whose temple was before the city" | "whose temple stood just outside the city" | "whose temple was before the city" -- follows LOGOS; pro tēs poleōs = before the city; PNEUMA adds "just outside" (interpretive) |
| 13 | "together-with the crowds to offer-sacrifice" vs. "to offer sacrifice with the crowds" vs. "he and the crowds wanted to offer sacrifices" | "together-with the crowds" | "with the crowds" | "he and the crowds" | "together with the crowds" -- follows EMET's emphasis on syn tois ochlois; communal action |
| 14 | Sentence structure | Two participles, then main verb | Temporal clause, then main verb | Three main clauses | Temporal clause -- follows LOGOS; smoother English |
| 15 | "Men" vs. "Friends" | "Men" (andres) | "Men" | "Friends" | "Men" -- 2-to-1; andres is standard Lukan address |
| 15 | "of-like-nature" vs. "of like nature" vs. "only human beings, just like you" | "of-like-nature" | "of like nature" | "only human beings, just like you" | "of like nature" -- follows LOGOS; homoiopatheis; see Council Notes |
| 15 | "vain-things" vs. "vain things" vs. "worthless things" | "vain-things" (mataiōn) | "vain things" | "worthless things" | "vain things" -- follows LOGOS; mataiōn = vain/futile; echoes LXX prophetic language |
| 16 | "allowed" vs. "permitted" vs. "let" | "allowed" (eiasen) | "permitted" | "let" | "permitted" -- follows LOGOS; eiasen = allowed/permitted; "permitted" carries more formal weight |
| 17 | "without-witness" vs. "without witness" vs. "without evidence of who he is" | "without-witness" | "without witness" | "without evidence of who he is" | "without witness" -- follows LOGOS; amartyron; see Council Notes |
| 17 | "doing-good, giving you rains" vs. "doing good and giving you rains" vs. "he shows kindness by giving you rain" | "doing-good, giving" | "doing good and giving" | "shows kindness by giving" | "doing good and giving" -- follows LOGOS; the two participles are coordinate |
| 18 | "with-difficulty" vs. "scarcely" vs. "barely managed" | "with-difficulty" (molis) | "scarcely" | "barely managed" | "scarcely" -- follows LOGOS; molis = scarcely/with difficulty |
| 18 | "not to sacrifice to them" vs. "from offering sacrifice to them" vs. "from sacrificing to them" | "not to sacrifice" (tou mē thuein) | "from offering sacrifice" | "from sacrificing" | "from offering sacrifice" -- follows LOGOS; the articular infinitive is rendered naturally |
| 19 | "came-upon [them]" vs. "came" vs. "arrived" | "came-upon" (epēlthan) | "came" | "arrived" | "came" -- follows LOGOS; the hostile sense is clear from context |
| 19 | "supposing him to have died" vs. "supposing him to be dead" vs. "leaving him for dead" | "supposing him to have died" | "supposing him to be dead" | "leaving him for dead" | "supposing him to be dead" -- follows LOGOS; see Council Notes |
| 20 | "having risen-up he entered" vs. "he rose up and entered" vs. "he got up and went back" | "having risen-up he entered" | "he rose up and entered" | "he got up and went back" | "he rose up and entered" -- follows LOGOS; anastas as temporal participle; PNEUMA's "got up" is too casual for the dramatic moment |
| 21 | "made-disciples [of] a considerable [number]" vs. "made many disciples" vs. "making a substantial number of disciples" | "made-disciples [of] a considerable [number]" | "made many disciples" | "making a substantial number of disciples" | "made many disciples" -- follows LOGOS; mathēteusantes hikanous; PNEUMA's "substantial number" is verbose |
| 22 | "remain in the faith" vs. "continue in the faith" vs. "hold firm in the faith" | "remain" (emmenein) | "continue" | "hold firm" | "continue" -- follows LOGOS; see Council Notes |
| 22 | "it-is-necessary [for] us" vs. "we must" vs. "We must" | "it-is-necessary [for] us" (dei hēmas) | "we must" | "We must" | "we must" -- follows LOGOS; dei = it is necessary; "we must" is natural English |
| **23** | **cheirotonēsantes ("having appointed") -- SIGNIFICANT** | "having appointed" | "having appointed" | "appointed" | **"having appointed"** -- unanimous on meaning; see detailed discussion below |
| 23 | "fastings" vs. "fasting" | "fastings" (nēsteiōn, plural) | "fasting" | "fasting" | "fasting" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); English "fasting" covers the plural sense |
| 23 | "committed" vs. "committed" vs. "entrusted" | "committed" | "committed" | "entrusted" | "committed" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); parethento = committed/entrusted; "committed" preserves the formal, liturgical sense |
| 26 | "delivered-over" vs. "commended" vs. "entrusted" | "delivered-over" (paradedomenoi) | "commended" | "entrusted" | "commended" -- follows LOGOS; see Council Notes |
| 26 | "from-where" vs. "from where" vs. "the very place where" | "from-where" | "from where" | "the very place where" | "from where" -- follows LOGOS; hothen rendered simply; PNEUMA's "the very place" adds emphasis not in Greek |
| 26 | "they fulfilled" vs. "they had fulfilled" vs. "they had now completed" | "they fulfilled" | "they had fulfilled" | "they had now completed" | "they had fulfilled" -- follows LOGOS; pluperfect rendering fits narrative sequence |
| 27 | "God did with them" vs. "God had done with them" vs. "God had done through them" | "God did with them" (met' autōn) | "God had done with them" | "God had done through them" | "God had done with them" -- follows LOGOS; meta + genitive = "with"; PNEUMA's "through" (dia) is a different preposition |
| 27 | "to the nations" vs. "to the Gentiles" | "to the nations" (tois ethnesin) | "to the Gentiles" | "for the Gentiles" | "to the Gentiles" -- follows LOGOS; in this context ethnē = Gentiles (Jew/Gentile contrast) |
| 28 | "not a little time" vs. "no little time" vs. "a considerable time" | "not a little" | "no little" | "a considerable" | "no little time" -- follows LOGOS; Lukan litotes preserved |

-- SOPHIA
